unit: Unit 15: Systems of Linear Differential Equations
title: Systems of Linear Differential Equations
subtitle: Decoupling, the Matrix Exponential, and Fundamental Matrices
cheatSubtitle: Condensed Cheat Sheet: Eigenvalue Decoupling, $e^{At}$, and Fundamental Matrices
description: Coupled first-order linear systems x' = Ax solved by the eigenvalue method. Higher-order equations are recast as first-order systems; a matrix with distinct eigenvalues is diagonalized A = PDP^{-1} so the modal substitution y = P^{-1}x turns the coupled system into the decoupled y' = Dy. The matrix exponential e^{At} = P e^{Dt} P^{-1} packages every mode into one operator and generalizes the scalar e^{at}; the fundamental matrix collects the independent solutions and reproduces e^{At} when normalized at t = 0. Complex eigenvalues give oscillatory real modes via Euler's formula.

atAGlance.title: Unit 15 at a Glance: Solving Coupled Linear Systems
atAGlance.summary: A coupled system $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ looks tangled only because the variables are written in the wrong basis. The eigenvectors of $A$ are the right basis: in those coordinates the matrix becomes diagonal, $A=PDP^{-1}$, and the substitution $\mathbf{y}=P^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ \emph{decouples} the system into independent scalar equations $\mathbf{y}'=D\mathbf{y}$. Packaging all of the modes into one operator gives the matrix exponential $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$, the exact matrix-valued analogue of the scalar $e^{at}$, whose columns are collected by the fundamental matrix.
bullets: \textbf{Higher order to first order} --- any $n$th-order linear ODE becomes a first-order system $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ through the state vector of derivatives.
\textbf{Decoupling by diagonalization} --- with distinct eigenvalues, $A=PDP^{-1}$ and $\mathbf{y}=P^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ split the system into independent modes $y_i'=\lambda_i y_i$.
\textbf{The matrix exponential} --- $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$ solves $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x},\ \mathbf{x}(0)=\mathbf{x}_0$ as $\mathbf{x}(t)=e^{At}\mathbf{x}_0$, generalizing scalar $e^{at}$.
\textbf{Complex eigenvalues} --- $\lambda=\alpha\pm i\beta$ produce spiraling real modes $e^{\alpha t}(\cos\beta t,\ \sin\beta t)$ through Euler's formula.

modules.title: Converting Higher-Order Equations to First-Order Systems
modules.topics: Recasting an $n$th-order linear ODE as a first-order system; the state vector of successive derivatives; the companion matrix; the unified matrix form $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$.

formulas.title: Companion Form of $y''+py'+qy=0$
formulas.body: \begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix}' = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -q & -p \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad x_1=y,\ x_2=y'.

methodology.title: Method --- Convert an $n$th-Order ODE to a System
steps: Name the state variables $x_1=y,\ x_2=y',\ \ldots,\ x_n=y^{(n-1)}$.
Differentiate each: the first $n-1$ rows are simply $x_i'=x_{i+1}$.
Solve the original ODE for the top derivative $y^{(n)}$ and write the last row $x_n'$ in terms of the $x_i$.
Collect the coefficients into the companion matrix $A$ and read off $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$.

examples.title: Worked Example --- A Second-Order ODE Becomes a System
steps: \text{Let } x_1 = y, \quad x_2 = y'. \text{ Then } x_1' = y' = x_2.
\begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix}' = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix}.
\text{Check: } \det(A-\lambda I) = \lambda^2 + 5\lambda + 6 = (\lambda+2)(\lambda+3) \text{ matches the ODE's characteristic polynomial.}

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body: Every higher-order linear ODE is secretly a first-order \textcolor{accentorange}{system}. The state vector stacks $y$ together with its derivatives, and the companion matrix's characteristic polynomial is \textcolor{accentorange}{exactly the characteristic equation of the original ODE} --- so the eigenvalue machinery and the scalar method agree.

warning.title: Common Pitfall
warning.body: When you solve for the top derivative, the coefficients enter the last row of $A$ with \textcolor{warnred}{flipped signs}: $y''=-6y-5y'$ gives the row $(-6,\,-5)$, not $(6,\,5)$. Forgetting the sign flip silently changes every eigenvalue.

modules.title: The Eigenvalue Method and Decoupling
modules.topics: The trial solution $\mathbf{x}=e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}$; the modal matrix $P$ of eigenvectors and the diagonal eigenvalue matrix $D$; the diagonalization $A=PDP^{-1}$; the modal substitution $\mathbf{y}=P^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ that turns $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ into the decoupled $\mathbf{y}'=D\mathbf{y}$.

formulas.title: Eigen-Ansatz and the General Solution
formulas.body: \mathbf{x}=e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}\ \Rightarrow\ A\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v}; \qquad \mathbf{x}(t)=c_1 e^{\lambda_1 t}\mathbf{v}_1 + c_2 e^{\lambda_2 t}\mathbf{v}_2.

formulas.title: Modal Matrix and Diagonalization
formulas.body: P = \big[\,\mathbf{v}_1 \mid \mathbf{v}_2\,\big], \quad D = \begin{pmatrix} \lambda_1 & 0 \\ 0 & \lambda_2 \end{pmatrix}: \qquad A = PDP^{-1}, \qquad D = P^{-1}AP.

formulas.title: The Decoupling Substitution
formulas.body: \mathbf{y}=P^{-1}\mathbf{x} \ \Rightarrow\ \mathbf{y}' = P^{-1}\mathbf{x}' = P^{-1}A\mathbf{x} = P^{-1}AP\,\mathbf{y} = D\mathbf{y}: \quad y_i' = \lambda_i y_i.

methodology.title: Method --- Decouple $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ into $\mathbf{y}'=D\mathbf{y}$
steps: Solve $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$ for the eigenvalues $\lambda_1,\lambda_2$ and find an eigenvector $\mathbf{v}_i$ for each.
Build the modal matrix $P=[\mathbf{v}_1\mid\mathbf{v}_2]$ from the eigenvectors as columns and the diagonal $D=\operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1,\lambda_2)$.
Compute $P^{-1}$ and confirm the diagonalization $P^{-1}AP=D$ (this is the algebraic heart of the decoupling).
Substitute $\mathbf{y}=P^{-1}\mathbf{x}$: the system collapses to the independent modes $y_i'=\lambda_i y_i$, solve each as $y_i=c_i e^{\lambda_i t}$, then return via $\mathbf{x}=P\mathbf{y}$.

examples.title: Worked Example --- Decoupling a System with Distinct Real Eigenvalues
examples.setup: Decouple $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ for $A=\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute the eigenvalues, the modal matrix $P$, its inverse $P^{-1}$, and verify $P^{-1}AP=D$ in full --- no skipped steps.
steps: \textbf{Eigenvalues: } \det(A-\lambda I)=\det\begin{pmatrix} 1-\lambda & 2 \\ 3 & 2-\lambda \end{pmatrix} = (1-\lambda)(2-\lambda) - (2)(3).
(1-\lambda)(2-\lambda) - 6 = \lambda^2 - 3\lambda + 2 - 6 = \lambda^2 - 3\lambda - 4 = (\lambda-4)(\lambda+1).
\lambda^2 - 3\lambda - 4 = 0 \ \Rightarrow\ \lambda_1 = 4, \quad \lambda_2 = -1.
\textbf{Eigenvector } \lambda_1=4:\ (A-4I)\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} -3 & 2 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0} \ \Rightarrow\ -3v_1 + 2v_2 = 0 \ \Rightarrow\ \mathbf{v}_1 = (2,\,3).
\textbf{Eigenvector } \lambda_2=-1:\ (A+I)\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 3 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0} \ \Rightarrow\ v_1 + v_2 = 0 \ \Rightarrow\ \mathbf{v}_2 = (1,\,-1).
\textbf{Modal matrix: } P = \big[\,\mathbf{v}_1 \mid \mathbf{v}_2\,\big] = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad D = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}.
\textbf{Inverse: } \det P = (2)(-1) - (1)(3) = -5, \qquad P^{-1} = \frac{1}{-5}\begin{pmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ -3 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix}.

\textbf{Verify } P^{-1}P=I:\ \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 \end{pmatrix} = I.\ \checkmark
\textbf{First product } AP = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 8 & -1 \\ 12 & 1 \end{pmatrix}.

\textbf{Then } P^{-1}(AP) = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 8 & -1 \\ 12 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 20 & 0 \\ 0 & -5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = D.\ \checkmark
\textbf{Decoupled system: } \mathbf{y}'=D\mathbf{y} \ \Rightarrow\ y_1'=4y_1,\ \ y_2'=-y_2 \ \Rightarrow\ y_1=c_1 e^{4t},\ y_2=c_2 e^{-t}.
\textbf{Back-substitute } \mathbf{x}=P\mathbf{y}:\ \mathbf{x}(t) = c_1 e^{4t}\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} + c_2 e^{-t}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}.

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body: Diagonalization \emph{is} decoupling. Writing $A=PDP^{-1}$ and changing to modal coordinates $\mathbf{y}=P^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ replaces the tangled $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ with $n$ \textcolor{accentorange}{independent scalar equations} $y_i'=\lambda_i y_i$. Each mode evolves as $e^{\lambda_i t}$ on its own eigenvector, and $\mathbf{x}=P\mathbf{y}$ reassembles them.

warning.title: Common Pitfall
warning.body: The eigenvectors are the \textcolor{warnred}{columns} of $P$, in the same order as the eigenvalues down the diagonal of $D$. Swapping a column without swapping the matching diagonal entry breaks $P^{-1}AP=D$. Diagonalization also \textcolor{warnred}{requires a full set of independent eigenvectors} --- a repeated eigenvalue short on eigenvectors is defective and needs the generalized-eigenvector route instead.

modules.title: The Matrix Exponential
modules.topics: The power-series definition of $e^{At}$; its closed form $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$ via diagonalization; $e^{Dt}=\operatorname{diag}(e^{\lambda_i t})$; why $e^{At}$ is the natural matrix generalization of the scalar $e^{at}$; the initial-value solution $\mathbf{x}(t)=e^{At}\mathbf{x}_0$.

formulas.title: Closed Form via Diagonalization
formulas.body: A = PDP^{-1} \ \Rightarrow\ e^{At} = P\,e^{Dt}\,P^{-1}, \qquad e^{Dt} = \begin{pmatrix} e^{\lambda_1 t} & 0 \\ 0 & e^{\lambda_2 t} \end{pmatrix}.

methodology.title: Method --- Compute $e^{At}$ by Diagonalization
steps: Diagonalize $A=PDP^{-1}$ using the eigenvectors (columns of $P$) and eigenvalues (diagonal of $D$).
Exponentiate the diagonal entrywise: $e^{Dt}=\operatorname{diag}(e^{\lambda_1 t},\,e^{\lambda_2 t})$, since the series of a diagonal matrix acts independently on each entry.
Reassemble $e^{At}=P\,e^{Dt}\,P^{-1}$ by multiplying the three matrices left to right.
Sanity check $e^{A\cdot 0}=I$, then read off the IVP solution $\mathbf{x}(t)=e^{At}\mathbf{x}_0$.

examples.title: Worked Example --- The Matrix Exponential of the Same $A$
examples.setup: Using the diagonalization $A=\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}=PDP^{-1}$ from 15.2 (with $P=\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$, $P^{-1}=\tfrac15\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix}$, $D=\operatorname{diag}(4,-1)$), build $e^{At}$ explicitly.
steps: \textbf{Exponentiate the diagonal: } e^{Dt} = \begin{pmatrix} e^{4t} & 0 \\ 0 & e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}.

\textbf{First product } P\,e^{Dt} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} e^{4t} & 0 \\ 0 & e^{-t} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t} & e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t} & -e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}.
\textbf{Then } e^{At} = \big(P\,e^{Dt}\big)P^{-1} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t} & e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t} & -e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix}.
e^{At} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t} + 3e^{-t} & 2e^{4t} - 2e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t} - 3e^{-t} & 3e^{4t} + 2e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}.
\textbf{Check } t=0:\ e^{A\cdot 0} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 2+3 & 2-2 \\ 3-3 & 3+2 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 \end{pmatrix} = I.\ \checkmark
\textbf{IVP solution: } \mathbf{x}(t) = e^{At}\mathbf{x}_0 \text{ delivers the trajectory through any } \mathbf{x}_0 \text{ in one stroke.}

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body: The matrix exponential is the \textcolor{accentorange}{single operator that advances the whole system in time}. Because $A$ and $P$ share eigenvectors, $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$ simply exponentiates each eigenvalue and reassembles --- the exact matrix analogue of $e^{at}$, satisfying $\frac{d}{dt}e^{At}=Ae^{At}$ and $e^{A\cdot 0}=I$, so $\mathbf{x}(t)=e^{At}\mathbf{x}_0$ solves the IVP outright.

warning.title: Common Pitfall
warning.body: $e^{At}$ is \textcolor{warnred}{not} the entrywise exponential of $At$ --- you must use the series or the diagonalization. And $e^{At}e^{Bt}=e^{(A+B)t}$ holds \textcolor{warnred}{only when $A$ and $B$ commute} ($AB=BA$); for non-commuting matrices that shortcut fails.

modules.title: Fundamental Matrices and the General Solution
modules.topics: The fundamental matrix $\Psi(t)$ whose columns are independent solutions; the Wronskian as its determinant; the compact general solution $\mathbf{x}=\Psi(t)\mathbf{c}$; the link $e^{At}=\Psi(t)\Psi(0)^{-1}$ that normalizes any fundamental matrix to the exponential.

formulas.title: Independence via the Wronskian
formulas.body: W(t) = \det\Psi(t) \neq 0 \ \text{ for some } t \ \Rightarrow\ \text{the columns are linearly independent solutions.}

methodology.title: Method --- Build and Use a Fundamental Matrix
steps: Find a full set of independent solutions $\mathbf{x}_i(t)=e^{\lambda_i t}\mathbf{v}_i$ from the eigenpairs.
Place them as the columns of $\Psi(t)=[\mathbf{x}_1\mid\mathbf{x}_2\mid\cdots]$ and confirm $\det\Psi\neq 0$.
Write the general solution compactly as $\mathbf{x}(t)=\Psi(t)\mathbf{c}$, fixing $\mathbf{c}$ from $\mathbf{x}(0)=\Psi(0)\mathbf{c}$.
If the transition operator is wanted, normalize: $e^{At}=\Psi(t)\Psi(0)^{-1}$.

examples.title: Worked Example --- Fundamental Matrix Recovers $e^{At}$
examples.setup: For $A=\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ with eigensolutions $e^{4t}(2,3)$ and $e^{-t}(1,-1)$, build $\Psi(t)$ and confirm $\Psi(t)\Psi(0)^{-1}=e^{At}$.
steps: \Psi(t) = \begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t} & e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t} & -e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}, \qquad W(t)=\det\Psi = -2e^{3t} - 3e^{3t} = -5e^{3t} \neq 0.
\Psi(0) = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = P, \qquad \Psi(0)^{-1} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix} = P^{-1}.

\Psi(t)\Psi(0)^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t} & e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t} & -e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}\cdot\frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t}+3e^{-t} & 2e^{4t}-2e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t}-3e^{-t} & 3e^{4t}+2e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}.
\text{This is exactly the } e^{At} \text{ of 15.3: a fundamental matrix normalized at } t=0 \text{ is the matrix exponential.}\ \checkmark

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body: The fundamental matrix is the \textcolor{accentorange}{bookkeeping device for an entire solution space}: its columns are independent solutions, $\det\Psi\neq 0$ guarantees they span, and $\mathbf{x}=\Psi\mathbf{c}$ is every solution at once. Normalizing it at $t=0$ recovers the matrix exponential, $e^{At}=\Psi(t)\Psi(0)^{-1}$ --- two languages for the same flow.

warning.title: Common Pitfall
warning.body: Any $\Psi$ with independent columns is \emph{a} fundamental matrix, but it equals $e^{At}$ \textcolor{warnred}{only when $\Psi(0)=I$}. If $\Psi(0)\neq I$ you must multiply by $\Psi(0)^{-1}$ on the right --- not the left. Order matters: $\Psi(t)\Psi(0)^{-1}$, never $\Psi(0)^{-1}\Psi(t)$.

modules.title: Complex Eigenvalues and Oscillatory Modes
modules.topics: Complex conjugate eigenvalues $\lambda=\alpha\pm i\beta$; extracting two real solutions from one complex eigensolution via Euler's formula; the real general solution as decaying or growing oscillations $e^{\alpha t}(\cos\beta t,\ \sin\beta t)$.

formulas.title: Complex Eigenpair and Euler Expansion
formulas.body: \lambda = \alpha \pm i\beta: \qquad e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v} = e^{\alpha t}\big(\cos\beta t + i\sin\beta t\big)\mathbf{v}.

formulas.title: Two Real Solutions from One Complex Mode

methodology.title: Method --- Solve a System with Complex Eigenvalues
steps: Find the complex eigenvalue $\lambda=\alpha+i\beta$ and a complex eigenvector $\mathbf{v}$ (use only one of the conjugate pair).
Form the complex solution $e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}$ and expand with $e^{i\beta t}=\cos\beta t + i\sin\beta t$.
Split into real and imaginary parts $\mathbf{x}_1=\operatorname{Re}$, $\mathbf{x}_2=\operatorname{Im}$ --- two independent real solutions.
Write the real general solution $\mathbf{x}=c_1\mathbf{x}_1 + c_2\mathbf{x}_2$ and read the spiral direction from $\operatorname{sign}(\alpha)$.

examples.title: Worked Example --- A Spiraling System
steps: \det(A-\lambda I) = (2-\lambda)^2 + 9 = \lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 13 = 0 \ \Rightarrow\ \lambda = 2 \pm 3i \quad (\alpha=2,\ \beta=3).
\lambda = 2+3i:\ (A-\lambda I)\mathbf{v} = \begin{pmatrix} -3i & -3 \\ 3 & -3i \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0} \ \Rightarrow\ \mathbf{v} = (1,\,-i).
e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v} = e^{2t}\big(\cos 3t + i\sin 3t\big)\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -i \end{pmatrix} = e^{2t}\begin{pmatrix} \cos 3t + i\sin 3t \\ \sin 3t - i\cos 3t \end{pmatrix}.
\mathbf{x}_1 = \operatorname{Re} = e^{2t}\begin{pmatrix} \cos 3t \\ \sin 3t \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{x}_2 = \operatorname{Im} = e^{2t}\begin{pmatrix} \sin 3t \\ -\cos 3t \end{pmatrix}.
\mathbf{x}(t) = e^{2t}\left[\, c_1\begin{pmatrix} \cos 3t \\ \sin 3t \end{pmatrix} + c_2\begin{pmatrix} \sin 3t \\ -\cos 3t \end{pmatrix} \right]: \quad \alpha=2>0 \Rightarrow \text{outward spiral.}

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body: A complex eigenpair encodes \textcolor{accentorange}{rotation plus scaling}. Euler's formula turns the single complex mode $e^{(\alpha+i\beta)t}\mathbf{v}$ into two real solutions whose $\cos\beta t$ and $\sin\beta t$ supply the oscillation and whose $e^{\alpha t}$ envelope sets the spiral: inward for $\alpha<0$, a closed center for $\alpha=0$, outward for $\alpha>0$.

warning.title: Common Pitfall
warning.body: Use \textcolor{warnred}{only one} eigenvalue of the conjugate pair --- taking both double-counts the solution space. And the real and imaginary parts are \emph{already} the two independent real solutions; do not also tack on the conjugate $\overline{\lambda}$ mode.

practice.subtitle: Practice Set: Problems and Complete Solutions
practice.intro: Work the following ten problems in order. They climb from converting a higher-order ODE into a first-order system, through the eigenvalue method and the full decoupling $A=PDP^{-1}$ with $P$, $P^{-1}$, and $D$ computed explicitly, into the matrix exponential $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$, the fundamental matrix and its link $e^{At}=\Psi(t)\Psi(0)^{-1}$, and a complex-eigenvalue capstone that produces a spiraling real solution.
problems: \textbf{(To a system)} Convert $y'' + 5y' + 6y = 0$ into a first-order system $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$, and state the companion matrix $A$.
\textbf{(Eigenvalues)} For $A=\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$, compute $\det(A-\lambda I)$ and find both eigenvalues.
\textbf{(Eigenvectors and $P$)} For that same $A$, find an eigenvector for each eigenvalue and assemble the modal matrix $P=[\mathbf{v}_1\mid\mathbf{v}_2]$.
\textbf{(Verify decoupling)} Confirm $P^{-1}AP=D$ for the $A$, $P$, $P^{-1}$ above, and state the decoupled system $\mathbf{y}'=D\mathbf{y}$.
\textbf{(General solution)} Write the general solution $\mathbf{x}(t)$ of $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ from the eigenpairs $\lambda_1=4,\ \mathbf{v}_1=(2,3)$ and $\lambda_2=-1,\ \mathbf{v}_2=(1,-1)$.
\textbf{(Matrix exponential)} Using $A=PDP^{-1}$ with $D=\operatorname{diag}(4,-1)$, build $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$ in closed form.
\textbf{(Fundamental matrix)} Write a fundamental matrix $\Psi(t)$ for this system and compute its Wronskian $\det\Psi(t)$.
\textbf{(Normalization)} Explain why $e^{At}=\Psi(t)\Psi(0)^{-1}$, and identify $\Psi(0)$ for the $\Psi$ above.
\textbf{(Complex capstone)} Solve $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ for $A=\begin{pmatrix} 2 & -3 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$, giving the real general solution and the spiral direction.
solutions:
\noindent\textbf{Solution 1.}\quad Let $x_1=y$, $x_2=y'$, so $x_1'=x_2$ and $x_2'=y''=-6y-5y'=-6x_1-5x_2$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix}' = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad A = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{pmatrix}.} \]
The coefficients enter the bottom row with flipped signs.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 2.}\quad Expand the determinant:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\det(A-\lambda I) = (1-\lambda)(2-\lambda) - (2)(3) = \lambda^2 - 3\lambda - 4 = (\lambda-4)(\lambda+1).} \]
So $\lambda_1=4$ and $\lambda_2=-1$.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 3.}\quad Solve $(A-\lambda I)\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}$ for each eigenvalue:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lambda=4:\ \begin{pmatrix} -3 & 2 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0} \Rightarrow \mathbf{v}_1=(2,3);} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lambda=-1:\ \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 3 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0} \Rightarrow \mathbf{v}_2=(1,-1).} \]
Hence $P=\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 4.}\quad The determinant and inverse are
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\det P = (2)(-1)-(1)(3) = -5, \qquad P^{-1} = \frac{1}{-5}\begin{pmatrix} -1 & -1 \\ -3 & 2 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & -2 \end{pmatrix}.} \]

\noindent\textbf{Solution 5.}\quad Multiply in two steps, $AP$ then $P^{-1}(AP)$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{AP = \begin{pmatrix} 8 & -1 \\ 12 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad P^{-1}AP = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 20 & 0 \\ 0 & -5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = D.} \]
The decoupled system is $y_1'=4y_1$, $y_2'=-y_2$.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 6.}\quad Each eigenpair contributes one exponential mode:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\mathbf{x}(t) = c_1 e^{4t}\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} + c_2 e^{-t}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}.} \]
With $\lambda_1=4>0>\lambda_2=-1$ the origin is a saddle.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 7.}\quad Exponentiate the diagonal and reassemble $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{Pe^{Dt} = \begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t} & e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t} & -e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}, \quad e^{At} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t}+3e^{-t} & 2e^{4t}-2e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t}-3e^{-t} & 3e^{4t}+2e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}.} \]
At $t=0$ this is $I$, as it must be.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 8.}\quad Stack the two eigensolutions as columns:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\Psi(t) = \begin{pmatrix} 2e^{4t} & e^{-t} \\ 3e^{4t} & -e^{-t} \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \det\Psi = -2e^{3t}-3e^{3t} = -5e^{3t} \neq 0.} \]
The nonzero Wronskian confirms the columns are independent.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 9.}\quad Both $\Psi(t)$ and $e^{At}$ solve $X'=AX$; they agree once normalized to the same value at $t=0$. Since $e^{A\cdot 0}=I$,
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{e^{At} = \Psi(t)\,\Psi(0)^{-1}, \qquad \Psi(0) = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = P.} \]
Right-multiplying by $\Psi(0)^{-1}$ forces the value $I$ at $t=0$.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 10.}\quad Here $\det(A-\lambda I)=\lambda^2-4\lambda+13=0$ gives $\lambda=2\pm 3i$. With $\mathbf{v}=(1,-i)$ for $\lambda=2+3i$, Euler's formula yields
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\mathbf{x}(t) = e^{2t}\left[ c_1\begin{pmatrix} \cos 3t \\ \sin 3t \end{pmatrix} + c_2\begin{pmatrix} \sin 3t \\ -\cos 3t \end{pmatrix} \right].} \]
Since $\alpha=2>0$, every trajectory spirals outward.

takeaway.title: Unit Key Takeaway
takeaway.body: One idea threads the whole unit: a linear system is easiest in its own eigenbasis. Recast higher-order ODEs as $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$, then \textcolor{accentorange}{diagonalize $A=PDP^{-1}$} so the modal substitution $\mathbf{y}=P^{-1}\mathbf{x}$ \textcolor{accentorange}{decouples} the system. The \textcolor{accentorange}{matrix exponential $e^{At}=Pe^{Dt}P^{-1}$} packages every mode into one operator and generalizes scalar $e^{at}$; the \textcolor{accentorange}{fundamental matrix} collects the same solutions and reproduces $e^{At}$ at $t=0$. Complex eigenvalues simply trade pure exponentials for spirals.
